steps.description: A mixture of CaHPO4, Ca(OH)2 and CaF2 was mixed with a certain amount of titania (20 wt%)
mechanically activated for 5 h, 10 h and 15 h respectively without using any process control agent (PCA).
all the milled specimens were filled in a quartz boat separately, and then were annealed under atmospheric pressure at 700 ℃ for 2 h.

human_recipe.notes: MA was performed at room temperature under air atmosphere using a high-energy planetary ball mill with polyamide-6 vials (125 ml in volume) and zirconia balls (20 mm in diameter). In all the samples, the weight ratio of ball-to-powder (BPR), total powder mass and rotational speed were 20:1, 6 g and 600 rpm, respectively. In the thermal cycle, the heating rate from room temperature up to the desired temperature was fixed at 10 ℃/min.

evaluation.reasoning: The extracted ontology captures the essential synthesis information for the fluorapatite-titania (FAp-TiO2) nanocomposite with good overall accuracy. The target compound, fluorapatite, is correctly identified, and all four precursor materials are included with the appropriate vendor information and purity specifications. In addition, the three principal synthesis stages (mixing, mechanical activation, and annealing) are represented in the correct logical order. Critical processing parameters are also extracted accurately, including the milling speed (600 rpm), ball-to-powder ratio (20:1), TiO2 content (20 wt%), annealing temperature (700 °C), annealing time (2 h), and heating rate (10 °C/min). The ontology also appropriately leaves the individual reagent amounts unspecified, since the source text reports only the total powder mass (6 g) rather than the mass of each precursor. Some minor issues exist: the milling duration in step 2 is set to 15 h (the maximum), but the paper describes three milling durations (5 h, 10 h, 15 h); the total powder mass of 6 g is not captured; the quartz boat is listed as equipment in the annealing step but is more of a sample holder than a furnace. Finally, characterization instruments are not included, but this omission is acceptable given that these instruments relate to post-synthesis characterization rather than the synthesis workflow. Overall, the extracted ontology is accurate and well-structured, with only minor omissions and simplifications that do not substantially affect its overall quality.
